target.title: fix(feishu): resolve exec/keychain appSecret SecretRefs on the outbound path
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: Issue #89338 reports that the Feishu **MEDIA** directive (and image/file uploads in general) silently fails when `appSecret` is configured as a `SecretRef` backed by an `exec`/Keychain provider. Normal chat (WebSocket connection) and text replies keep working; only the media upload is dropped, so the recipient receives no image. The regression appeared after migrating credentials from plaintext to a Keychain-backed `SecretRef`.
- **Root Cause**: Feishu resolves app credentials per outbound API call through `resolveFeishuRuntimeAccount` → `resolveFeishuSecretLike`, a **synchronous** resolver that only understands plaintext strings and `env`-source SecretRefs. For an `exec`/`file`/Keychain `SecretRef` it throws `FeishuSecretRefUnavailableError` (its own message says the value must be *resolved against an active gateway runtime snapshot before reading it*). The WebSocket connection and text replies receive a config in which `appSecret` was already resolved to a string, so they work; the media-upload delivery path can reach `feishuOutbound` with a config in which `appSecret` is still an unresolved `SecretRef`. The sync resolver then throws, the throw is caught in `feishuOutbound.sendMedia` (and the local-image path in `sendText`), and the send degrades to a plain URL/text fallback — the "silent" failure the reporter saw. Plaintext worked because a plaintext value is returned immediately, bypassing SecretRef resolution entirely.
- **Fix**: Resolve still-unresolved app-credential SecretRefs at the Feishu outbound seam before the SDK client is built. A new helper `resolveFeishuOutboundCredentialsConfig` inspects the outbound account's `appId`/`appSecret`; if either is still a `SecretRef`, it first reuses the value the gateway already resolved into the active runtime snapshot (`getRuntimeConfigSnapshot`) — so an `exec`/Keychain secret resolved at connection time is not re-run per send — and only falls back to the async, exec/Keychain-capable SDK resolver (`resolveConfiguredSecretInputString`) for a ref the snapshot does not cover (e.g. a standalone CLI process). The resolved string is inlined into a shallow copy of the config (at the account level when an accounts map exists, top-level otherwise, so an implicit single-account config does not gain a spurious accounts map). When inlining at the account level it patches the **existing matched account-map key** (which may be noncanonical / mixed-case) rather than the normalized id, so the account's other per-account settings are not shadowed by a new entry. Plaintext/already-resolved configs are returned unchanged with no SDK secret resolver call and no clone. The three `feishuOutbound` entry points (`sendPayload`, `sendText`, `sendMedia`) and the agent message-tool `send`/`thread-reply` action handler (`channel.ts`, covering its card / media / text branches) call it first, so the existing synchronous downstream resolution now sees a resolved string and the send proceeds.
- **What changed**:
  - `extensions/feishu/src/accounts.ts` — add `resolveFeishuOutboundCredentialsConfig` (and a small `resolveFeishuCredentialRefValue` helper) that resolves unresolved `appId`/`appSecret` SecretRefs — preferring the gateway runtime snapshot (`openclaw/plugin-sdk/runtime-config-snapshot`), then the async `openclaw/plugin-sdk/secret-input-runtime` resolver — and inlines them into the returned config at the existing matched account-map key. Also extract `findFeishuAccountMapKey` (shared with `resolveRawFeishuAccountConfig`) for the case-insensitive key match.
  - `extensions/feishu/src/outbound.ts` — resolve credentials at the start of the `feishuOutbound` `sendPayload`, `sendText`, and `sendMedia` handlers before any SDK client is built.
  - `extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts` — resolve credentials once at the start of the message-tool `send`/`thread-reply` action handler and use the resolved config for the card, media, and text branches (the card/text branches do not go through the outbound bridge).
  - `extensions/feishu/src/accounts.test.ts` / `channel.test.ts` — regression cases on the real resolver: the strict runtime account throws on an exec/Keychain `appSecret` SecretRef and succeeds after the helper inlines it; an inherited top-level SecretRef is resolved without inventing an accounts map; the matched noncanonical/mixed-case account key and its per-account settings are preserved; plaintext credentials return the same config object unchanged; an active runtime snapshot value is reused without the async resolver; and a message-tool text `send` with an exec SecretRef reaches `sendMessageFeishu` with the resolved credential.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - No config surface (no schema, defaults, doctor migrations, or `docs/reference/config`); the on-disk `SecretRef` shape is read, never rewritten.
  - No plugin SDK surface (no SDK exports, manifest, `extensions/*` api/runtime-api, registry/loader); the helper only consumes existing public SDK subpaths (`openclaw/plugin-sdk/runtime-config-snapshot`, `openclaw/plugin-sdk/secret-input-runtime`), both already used by other bundled plugins.
  - No dependency/lockfile changes. Gateway protocol unchanged.
  - The synchronous resolver, its error type, the connection/event-secret resolution, and the existing transient-error fallback are untouched; only the outbound delivery path gains an up-front resolution step.

### Reproduction

1. Configure a Feishu account with `appSecret` as an `exec`/Keychain `SecretRef`, e.g. `channels.feishu.accounts.<id>.appSecret = { "source": "exec", "provider": "keychain_feishu_<id>", "id": "value" }`.
2. Restart the gateway; WebSocket chat connects normally.
3. Send a local image via the `MEDIA` directive in a Feishu DM.
4. **Before this PR**: the upload throws `FeishuSecretRefUnavailableError`, is caught, and degrades to a URL/text fallback — no image is delivered.
5. **After this PR**: the SecretRef is resolved against the secret runtime before the client is built, and the image is uploaded normally.

Deterministic source reproduction (added as regression tests): with the reporter's exact `exec` SecretRef shape, `resolveFeishuRuntimeAccount` throws; after `resolveFeishuOutboundCredentialsConfig`, the same call resolves with the inlined secret.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed (#89338 — Feishu media upload now works with an exec/Keychain SecretRef appSecret)**: outbound delivery resolves unresolved app-credential SecretRefs before building the Feishu client, instead of dropping the upload to a URL/text fallback.

**Real environment tested (Linux, Node — Vitest against the real `extensions/feishu/src/accounts.ts` resolution path, with the SDK secret resolver mocked to emulate the gateway secret runtime; no live Feishu tenant)**: verified via `accounts.test.ts`, plus extension production and test type-checks and a format check on the changed files. A live Feishu media send against a real tenant was not run.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch (repo-root)**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/{accounts,channel,outbound}.test.ts`; `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json` and `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json`; `oxfmt --check` and `oxlint` on the changed files.

**Evidence after fix (Vitest output for the touched test files)**:

```text
 accounts.test.ts   Tests  28 passed (28)
 channel.test.ts    Tests  62 passed (62)
 outbound.test.ts   Tests  42 passed (42)
```

The new cases prove the regression and its fix on the real resolver: the strict runtime account throws `FeishuSecretRefUnavailableError` on an exec/Keychain `appSecret` SecretRef and then resolves successfully once `resolveFeishuOutboundCredentialsConfig` has inlined it; an inherited top-level SecretRef resolves without inventing an accounts map; the matched noncanonical/mixed-case account key keeps its per-account settings; plaintext credentials return the identical config object (no clone, no SDK secret resolver call); a value already present in the active runtime snapshot is reused without invoking the async resolver; and a message-tool text `send` with an exec SecretRef reaches `sendMessageFeishu` with the resolved credential. (The two test files mock exactly the SDK seams the helper uses — `openclaw/plugin-sdk/secret-input-runtime` and `runtime-config-snapshot` — which is the helper's real public boundary.)

**Observed result after fix (media path no longer drops the upload)**: `resolveFeishuRuntimeAccount` on the resolved config returns `configured: true` with the resolved `appSecret`, so the SDK client builds and the upload proceeds. Extension prod + test type-checks pass; oxlint and oxfmt are clean.

**What was not tested (live Feishu tenant media send)**: a real Feishu DM image send over a live WebSocket session with a Keychain-backed secret was not reproduced; the proof is the deterministic module-level reproduction the issue describes.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: an unresolved-credential outbound send now performs an async secret resolution. **Mitigation**: the resolver is only invoked when `appId`/`appSecret` is still a `SecretRef`; plaintext and already-resolved configs short-circuit with no resolver call and no clone, so the common path is unchanged. When a credential is genuinely unresolvable, the helper leaves the config untouched and the existing transient-error fallback still applies.
- **Risk**: an exec/Keychain secret could be re-run per send. **Mitigation**: the helper first reuses the gateway's already-resolved runtime snapshot (`getRuntimeConfigSnapshot`), so a secret resolved at connection time is not re-executed per send; the async provider resolver is only reached for a ref the snapshot does not cover (e.g. a standalone CLI process without an active gateway snapshot). This reuses an existing prepared runtime object rather than adding new cache state.
- **Risk**: config rewriting could change account selection or drop per-account settings. **Mitigation**: the resolved value is inlined at the existing matched account-map key (preserving a noncanonical/mixed-case key and its sibling settings), or top-level when no account entry matches; the original config object is never mutated. Covered by the regression tests.

### Out of scope / known siblings

This PR fixes the Feishu media path reported in #89338. The underlying shape — a channel resolving its outbound app credentials *per send* through a resolver that does not cover `exec`/keychain `SecretRef`s — is not necessarily unique to Feishu: channels that re-resolve outbound credentials at send time (rather than capturing a resolved token at connection) are worth auditing for the same class of failure (not necessarily silent) once their credentials move to an `exec`/keychain `SecretRef`. The structurally complete fix would be a cross-channel, gateway-outbound-layer unified `SecretRef` pre-resolution — a larger, design-first change. This PR intentionally scopes to the reported Feishu media path and does not change sibling channels.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Channels (Feishu)

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #89338

target.title: feat(cron): configurable minimum interval guardrail for recurring jobs
target.body:
## Summary

Adds an opt-in `cron.minInterval` setting that enforces a **minimum interval between fires for recurring cron jobs**. Creating or editing an `every`/`cron` job whose tightest interval falls below the configured floor is rejected with a clear error.

Motivation: enforcing a minimum cron frequency is a guardrail that belongs in an exposed OpenClaw setting rather than in each downstream backend. This mirrors the existing `cron.maxConcurrentRuns` operator guardrail and gives one enforcement point that covers every creation path (agent tool, CLI, gateway RPC).

## What changed

- **Config surface**: new `cron.minInterval` (`CronConfig` type, zod schema + duration validation, help text, label, docs). Accepts a duration string (`30s`, `5m`, `1h`) or a number of milliseconds; bare numbers are milliseconds. Default `0`/unset = no limit.
- **Resolver**: `resolveCronMinIntervalMs()` in `src/config/cron-limits.ts`, directly paralleling `resolveCronMaxConcurrentRuns()`.
- **Enforcement**: `assertScheduleMeetsMinInterval()` in the cron service, called from `createJob` (add) and from `update` when the schedule changes.
  - `every` jobs: `everyMs` compared to the floor.
  - `cron` expressions: judged by the **smallest gap** across a few sampled consecutive fires, so irregular bursts like `0,1 * * * *` are caught (croner supports seconds, so `* * * * * *` = 1s).
  - One-shot `at` jobs are exempt; a floor of `0` disables the guardrail.
- **Scope decisions** (compatibility-safe): default off so existing fast jobs keep working; updates only re-validate when the schedule itself changes, so unrelated edits to a pre-existing fast job are not blocked; manual/forced runs are unaffected.
- **Surfacing**: the rejection maps to `INVALID_REQUEST` in the gateway cron handler, and the cron tool description tells the agent to increase the interval instead of retrying.

## Config example

```json5
{
  cron: {
    minInterval: "5m", // reject recurring jobs that fire more often than every 5 minutes
  },
}
```

Error shape: `cron schedule fires every 30s, below the minimum interval of 5m`.

## Verification

Run locally (clean):

- `oxfmt --check` on all touched files — clean
- `oxlint` on touched files — clean
- `tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json` and `-p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json` — exit 0
- `check-import-cycles` — 0 runtime value cycles
- Vitest (targeted):
  - `src/config/cron-limits.test.ts`, `src/config/zod-schema.cron-min-interval.test.ts`
  - `src/cron/service/jobs.min-interval.test.ts` (every/cron/at, boundary, irregular-gap, create-path)
  - Regression: `src/cron/service.jobs.test.ts`, `jobs.apply-patch.test.ts`, `ops.test.ts`, `server-cron.test.ts`, `server.cron.test.ts`, `schema.help.quality.test.ts`, `schema.hints.test.ts`, `validation.allowed-values.test.ts`, `defaults.test.ts`, `tools-invoke-http.cron-regression.test.ts`, `cron-tool.test.ts` — all green

Broader changed-gate / Crabbox proof not yet run (draft).

## Release note

feat(cron): add `cron.minInterval` to set a minimum interval for recurring cron jobs as a guardrail against accidental high-frequency schedules. Thanks @amknight (requested by Tom Wood).

target.title: fix #80213: [Feature]: Skill author-defined setup hook (run skill-supplied script on install/update)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #80213.

- Keep skill-defined setup hooks disabled by default for ClawHub, upload, source, and gateway installer flows.
- Add an explicit `allowSetupHooks` / `--allow-setup-hooks` opt-in through the gateway protocol, gateway handlers, CLI, and install/update lifecycle paths.
- Document setup hook trust, host-owned timeout, failure, minimal hook env, and staging semantics, with regression coverage for default-skip and explicit opt-in behavior.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Skill packages can declare `metadata.openclaw.setup.script`, but OpenClaw no longer runs downloaded or installed skill setup hooks by default. Trusted admin/gateway/CLI callers must explicitly opt in, and the opt-in path confines setup scripts while giving hooks only minimal host context.
- **Real environment tested:** Existing PR repair worktree `/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-80266`; Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64; Node 22.22.0; repository pnpm/tooling; evidence under `/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-80266-evidence`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran the gateway proof script with `pnpm exec node --import tsx "$EVIDENCE_DIR/gateway-skills-install-setup-proof.ts"`; reran current-head frontmatter setup metadata tests, setup/install lifecycle tests, CLI/gateway/upload tests, production and test type checks, changed-file formatting, docs formatting, diff whitespace, public `setup.timeoutMs` contract search, and non-doc/lockfile diff-size count.
- **Evidence after fix:** `/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-80266-evidence/gateway-skills-install-setup-proof-current-head.log`; `/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-80266-evidence/gateway-skills-install-setup-proof-result.json`; `/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-80266-evidence/vitest-setup-env-gateway-current-head.log`; `/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-80266-evidence/timeout-contract-verification-current-head.log`; `/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-80266-evidence/tsgo-core-prod-current-head.log`; `/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-80266-evidence/tsgo-core-test-current-head-after-rebase.log`; earlier broader proof logs remain in `/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-80266-evidence/verification-summary.md`.
- **Observed result after fix:** Current-head gateway proof reports `defaultGateSkippedHook: true`, `explicitOptInHookRan: true`, `hookKind: "install"`, `configuredEnvBlocked: true`, `gatewayTokenBlocked: true`, and `nodeOptionsBlocked: true`; setup hook env is limited to host `PATH`, `SKILL_DIR`, and `OPENCLAW_HOOK_KIND`. Timeout-contract verification passed frontmatter setup metadata coverage (16 tests), lifecycle setup/install coverage (65 tests), type checking, formatting, docs formatting, `git diff --check`, and confirmed no public `setup.timeoutMs` contract references. Focused CLI/gateway/upload coverage passed 5 files / 92 tests. Non-doc/lockfile diff size is 726 changed lines, below the `size: XL` threshold of 1000.
- **What was not tested:** A live published ClawHub skill containing setup metadata was not installed from the public registry. This repair uses the real local gateway upload install path plus lifecycle/CLI/gateway handler tests, and it keeps live ClawHub setup execution behind explicit opt-in rather than default autorun.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** Medium-high for updating this existing PR. The code path that previously changed the trust boundary is now explicitly gated, the public gateway/CLI opt-in is wired, and local gateway/lifecycle/CLI evidence proves default-skip and opt-in behavior. Confidence is not marked high only because a live published ClawHub registry install was not available in this environment.
- **Patch quality notes:** The warnings around timeout/fallback/catch handling are part of the new setup-hook execution boundary: setup scripts are external process/file inputs, so the runner must report parse/read/execute failures, keep timeout policy host-owned, and support `sh` for non-executable shell scripts. These are not used to mask the trust-boundary issue; the root behavior change is the explicit opt-in gate across ClawHub, upload, source, gateway, and CLI entry points. The test-only type narrowing in ClawHub assertions checks whether the install transaction received an `afterInstall` callback without changing production types.
- **Target test file:** `src/skills/lifecycle/setup.test.ts`, `src/skills/lifecycle/archive-install.test.ts`, `src/skills/lifecycle/install.test.ts`, `src/skills/lifecycle/source-install.test.ts`, `src/skills/lifecycle/clawhub.test.ts`, `src/skills/loading/frontmatter.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/skills-upload.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/skills.clawhub.test.ts`, `src/cli/skills-cli.commands.test.ts`

## Why it matters / User impact

Skill authors requested a setup hook so installed skills can bootstrap local files or dependencies. Without this patch, OpenClaw either cannot run that declared setup path at install/update time or risks doing so implicitly in downloaded skill flows. The user-visible behavior after this patch is deliberate: normal installs/updates remain safe by default, while trusted CLI/gateway callers can opt in when they intentionally accept the local script execution boundary.

## Public contract and compatibility scope

- **Canonical scope:** `metadata.openclaw.setup.script` is the only skill-authored setup metadata in this PR, and `allowSetupHooks` / `--allow-setup-hooks` is the caller/admin opt-in that controls execution. `setup.timeoutMs` is intentionally not part of the public SKILL.md contract; setup timeout policy remains host-owned.
- **Default behavior:** setup hooks are parsed but not executed unless the caller opts in, so existing install/update flows do not start running downloaded scripts by default.
- **Related PR scan:** ClawSweeper flagged a competing draft admin-only plugin/RPC implementation and a published ClawHub plugin. This branch keeps the core install/update opt-in path under review and leaves the final product boundary choice to maintainers.
- **Out of scope:** no live public ClawHub registry setup-hook install was exercised here, no protocol version bump is made, no default autorun path is added, and this PR does not decide whether a plugin/RPC-only design should supersede the core path.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** `merge-risk: compatibility`, `merge-risk: security-boundary`, plus availability/session-state/message-delivery/auth-provider-adjacent signals from gateway and install/update paths.
- **Risk explanation:** The PR adds a public setup metadata shape and an opt-in execution path for skill-supplied scripts on the gateway/CLI host. That can affect install/update failure behavior, local process execution, minimal hook env semantics, gateway handler contracts, and docs/schema expectations.
- **Why acceptable:** The risky behavior is disabled by default, requires an explicit opt-in on each trusted caller path, uses realpath confinement for setup scripts, keeps setup timeout policy host-owned, and constructs setup child env from only `PATH`, `SKILL_DIR`, and `OPENCLAW_HOOK_KIND` through the shared host exec sanitizer.

## Regression Test Plan

- `pnpm exec node --import tsx "$EVIDENCE_DIR/gateway-skills-install-setup-proof.ts"`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --reporter verbose src/skills/loading/frontmatter.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.unit.config.ts --reporter verbose src/skills/lifecycle/source-install.test.ts src/skills/lifecycle/clawhub.test.ts src/skills/lifecycle/setup.test.ts src/skills/lifecycle/install.test.ts src/skills/lifecycle/archive-install.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --reporter verbose src/cli/skills-cli.commands.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/skills-upload.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/skills.clawhub.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-current-head.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test-current-head.tsbuildinfo`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/cli/skills-cli.commands.test.ts src/cli/skills-cli.ts src/gateway/server-methods/skills.clawhub.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/skills.ts src/skills/lifecycle/archive-install.test.ts src/skills/lifecycle/archive-install.ts src/skills/lifecycle/clawhub.ts src/skills/lifecycle/install.test.ts src/skills/lifecycle/install.ts src/skills/lifecycle/setup.test.ts src/skills/lifecycle/setup.ts src/skills/lifecycle/source-install.ts src/skills/lifecycle/upload-install.ts src/skills/loading/frontmatter.test.ts`
- `node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check`
- `git diff --check`

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** The original setup-hook implementation exposed a new executable skill metadata surface while enabling the ClawHub install/update path to run downloaded setup scripts by default. At the same time, the public gateway/CLI contract did not consistently expose one deliberate trust-boundary opt-in for the workflow described by the issue, so the risky default path and the intended admin-controlled path diverged.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The patch makes the trust decision explicit and consistent at the source of the behavior. Setup hooks remain parsed and executable only after the caller opts in through `allowSetupHooks` or `--allow-setup-hooks`; that same decision is propagated through gateway protocol validation, gateway handlers, CLI commands, ClawHub install/update, upload installs, source installs, and local installer execution. The added docs and tests cover both sides of the invariant: default installs/updates do not run setup hooks, and trusted opt-in installs/updates run them with realpath confinement plus minimal hook env.

## Review findings addressed

- **RF-001 (`status: waiting on author`):** This refresh updates the branch with current-head proof; the label itself is bot/maintainer-owned.
- **RF-002 (OpenGrep skill env host injection):** Addressed in current head. Setup hooks no longer consume configured skill env or `requires.env` values at all; they build child env from only `PATH`, `SKILL_DIR`, and `OPENCLAW_HOOK_KIND` through `sanitizeHostExecEnv`, while normal skill runtime env overrides keep the shared host-env sanitizer hardening.
- **RF-003 (new public metadata / CLI / gateway contract):** Maintainer decision required. This refresh narrows the skill-authored metadata to `metadata.openclaw.setup.script` only, intentionally leaves setup timeout policy host-owned, and keeps the remaining execution contract explicit, documented, and opt-in rather than adding a hidden default execution path.
- **RF-004 (gateway-host script execution trust boundary):** Maintainer security-boundary approval required. The automated repair keeps execution disabled by default and confines env/path/script handling; it does not hide that this is a deliberate trust-boundary change.
- **RF-005 (competing plugin/RPC boundary):** Maintainer product-boundary decision required. This PR preserves the core install/update opt-in implementation because that is the branch under review.
- **RF-006 (remaining action is maintainer API/security approval):** Confirmed. No narrower automated code repair is available beyond the explicit opt-in and sanitizer proof already included.
- **RF-007 (real behavior proof):** Current-head proof is recorded in `gateway-skills-install-setup-proof-current-head.log`, `gateway-skills-install-setup-proof-result.json`, and `timeout-contract-verification-current-head.log`.
- **RF-008 (reviewer real behavior proof request):** Addressed with local gateway upload/install proof plus current-head lifecycle, CLI, gateway, typecheck, format, docs, and public-contract-search verification. A live published ClawHub registry skill with setup metadata was still not available in this environment and remains disclosed above.
- **RF-009 (real behavior proof):** Addressed with current-head real gateway upload/install proof showing default setup hooks are skipped, explicit opt-in runs the hook, and configured/sensitive env is blocked; supporting Vitest, typecheck, format, docs, diff-check, and size-count evidence is listed in the Real behavior proof section.

target.title: fix(usage): exclude untimestamped records from daily ranges
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Fixes ranged dashboard/session usage summaries so untimestamped cached transcript rows do not inflate selected-day totals or model usage.
- Keeps all-time cache summaries intact, but forces finite date ranges to derive from timestamped transcript rows only.

Why does this matter now?

- Issue #89709 reports that after v2026.5.28, Dashboard "今日模型调用" can show historical cumulative model usage for a selected day.

What is the intended outcome?

- A selected-day usage range only includes transcript usage rows with timestamps inside that range.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- No UI layout changes, provider pricing changes, Discord changes, memory-core changes, Mattermost changes, or MCP changes.

What does success look like?

- Ranged usage summaries exclude untimestamped historical records from total tokens, message counts, tool usage, model usage, daily breakdowns, and daily model usage.

What should reviewers focus on?

- The cache-derived session summary path in `src/infra/session-cost-usage.ts` and the deterministic regression fixture in `src/infra/session-cost-usage.test.ts`.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #89709

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #87222

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- ClawSweeper marked #89709 as source-repro, queueable-fix, and fix-shape-clear.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Selected-day Dashboard/session usage ranges no longer include untimestamped historical cached transcript records in model totals.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on branch `codex/89709-daily-usage-range`, using the actual `refreshCostUsageCache` and `loadSessionCostSummaryFromCache` runtime functions with a temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran `node --import tsx -` with a fixture containing an untimestamped historical `openai/glm-5` assistant usage row for 1000 tokens, an out-of-range dated `gpt-5.5` row for 10 tokens, and selected-day `gpt-5.5` usage for 20 tokens, then loaded the range `2026-02-05T00:00:00.000Z` through `2026-02-05T23:59:59.999Z` from the cache.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```json
{
  "cacheStatus": "fresh",
  "totalTokens": 20,
  "totalCost": 0.02,
  "messageCounts": {
    "total": 2,
    "user": 1,
    "assistant": 1,
    "toolCalls": 1,
    "toolResults": 0,
    "errors": 0
  },
  "modelUsage": [
    "openai/gpt-5.5:20"
  ],
  "dailyModelUsage": [
    "2026-02-05/openai/gpt-5.5:20"
  ],
  "dailyBreakdown": [
    {
      "date": "2026-02-05",
      "tokens": 20,
      "cost": 0.02
    }
  ]
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The ranged cache summary reports only the selected-day `gpt-5.5` usage; the untimestamped `glm-5` 1000-token historical record is absent from `totalTokens`, `messageCounts`, `modelUsage`, and `dailyModelUsage`.
- What was not tested: I did not run a browser Dashboard session against a reporter WSL2 profile or their real provider console data.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The reporter's actual local cache was not available, so the proof uses a deterministic temp state dir that exercises the same source-proven cache aggregation path.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The prior source path only skipped out-of-range rows when `timestamp` existed, so untimestamped cached rows could still be added to ranged `totals` and `modelUsage`.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/session-cost-usage.test.ts --reporter=dot`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/session-cost-usage.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/usage.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/usage.sessions-usage.test.ts --reporter=dot`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/views/usage.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/usage-render-overview.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/usage.node.test.ts --reporter=dot`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/infra/session-cost-usage.ts src/infra/session-cost-usage.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/infra/session-cost-usage.ts src/infra/session-cost-usage.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `git diff --check`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Updated the cached session range regression to include an untimestamped historical model usage record and assert the selected-day summary only contains the timestamped selected-day model.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The same cache-derived range path could include untimestamped records in aggregate totals and `modelUsage` because timestamp filtering only rejected entries with timestamps outside the range.

If no test was added, why not?

- A focused regression test was added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Date-filtered usage totals and model usage now exclude untimestamped records.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Ranged usage summaries for legacy cached transcript rows without timestamps.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The change is limited to finite-range cache derivation. All-time summaries still include untimestamped entries, and regression coverage exercises the exact selected-day leak shape.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Maintainer review and ClawSweeper review.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- CI and ClawSweeper verdict.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Addresses ClawSweeper's source-repro notes on #89709 for untimestamped cached usage leaking into ranged model totals.

target.title: fix(compaction): bound stale transcript usage
target.body:
Fixes #81178

## Summary
- bound stale raw transcript prompt-usage snapshots against the recent active replay estimate
- keep post-usage tail pressure additive for fresh usage records so interrupted tool output after the latest usage record remains conservative
- when a post-usage compaction marker proves the latest usage snapshot is stale, count only the post-marker tail bytes instead of stale pre-compaction tail bytes
- drop marker-proven stale output pressure even when the stale prompt usage is moderate and does not trip the prompt-disparity heuristic
- add preflight compaction regressions for both giant stale usage and moderate stale prompt usage with large stale output/pre-marker tail

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: stale pre-compaction transcript usage, stale pre-marker tail bytes, and stale output tokens should not force another preflight compaction when a post-usage compaction marker proves that the active post-compaction replay is small and no explicit transcript-byte policy is exceeded.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source runtime from PR head `74ba2e34ec` on macOS, using a real temporary JSONL session transcript and the actual `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` path. The compact dependency was instrumented only to count whether the runtime attempted compaction.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: from the PR checkout at `74ba2e34ec`, ran a `node --import tsx` source-runtime proof that wrote a transcript with stale assistant usage `input=9000`, stale assistant usage `output=80000`, a 450000-byte stale pre-marker tool result, a compaction marker, and a small post-compaction replay; then it invoked `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` with a 100000-token context window.

Evidence after fix: copied terminal output from the direct source-runtime proof:

```json
{
  "head": "74ba2e34ec",
  "behavior": "post-usage compaction marker drops stale output and pre-marker tail pressure",
  "staleUsageInputTokens": 9000,
  "staleOutputTokens": 80000,
  "preMarkerTailBytes": 450000,
  "contextWindowTokens": 100000,
  "compactCalls": 0,
  "phaseCalls": [],
  "returnedOriginalEntry": true
}
```

Observed result after fix: the source-runtime preflight call returned the original session entry, never entered the `preflight_compacting` phase, and made zero compaction calls despite stale output plus stale pre-marker tail pressure that would otherwise exceed the configured 100k context window.

What was not tested: I did not run a live Discord/Pi provider session because the failure is in the local preflight estimator and the source-runtime proof exercises that estimator path directly.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts` (25 passed)
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `git diff --cached --check`
- `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD origin/main`
- `node --import tsx -e ...` source-runtime proof copied above

---

_Re-verified at exact branch head `74ba2e34ec` on 2026-06-03 (the earlier proof referenced `b90c31584d`, the head before the Dependency Guard rebase; the patch is unchanged — two commits on top of current `main` `ed4c4afc0f`)._

**Before fix** (origin/main estimator, same standalone source-runtime harness and transcript): `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` **throws** `Preflight compaction required but failed: not_compacted` — i.e. main forces preflight compaction for the stale-after-compaction transcript (the bug).

**After fix** (PR head `74ba2e34ec`), both regression scenarios via the real source-runtime path (`node --import tsx`, compaction action instrumented only to count attempts):

```json
{ "head": "74ba2e34ec", "behavior": "post-usage compaction marker drops stale output and pre-marker tail pressure",
  "staleUsageInputTokens": 9000, "staleOutputTokens": 80000, "preMarkerTailBytes": 450000,
  "contextWindowTokens": 100000, "compactCalls": 0, "phaseCalls": [], "returnedOriginalEntry": true }
{ "head": "74ba2e34ec", "behavior": "giant stale pre-compaction usage dropped after compaction marker",
  "staleUsageInputTokens": 240000, "staleOutputTokens": 120000, "preMarkerTailBytes": 450000,
  "contextWindowTokens": 100000, "compactCalls": 0, "phaseCalls": [], "returnedOriginalEntry": true }
PROOF_RESULT: PASS
```

Focused suite at this head: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts` → 42 passed.


---

### Update 2026-06-03 — addressed ClawSweeper P1 (head `26d1293708`)

ClawSweeper re-review (proof judged `diamond lobster`) raised a P1 patch-quality
risk: `transcriptLineHasPostUsageCompactionMarker` also matched the post-compaction
refresh phrases (`[Post-compaction context refresh]`, `Session was just compacted.`)
in free message text, so ordinary user/tool content echoing them could masquerade
as a marker and wrongly drop stale-usage pressure.

Fix: detect **only structured compaction records** (`type`/`payload.type` ===
`"compaction"` | `"session.compacted"` — the records the runtime actually persists
via `transcript-file-state.ts` / `session-manager.ts`). Dropped the free-text
fallback and the now-unused `collectTranscriptText` helper (net -? prod LOC).
The refresh phrases come from `post-compaction-context.ts` and are prompt-injected
context, not persisted transcript markers, so structured detection fully covers the
real signal.

Real behavior proof at head `26d1293708` (`node --import tsx`, real
`runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded`, compaction instrumented to count attempts):

```text
# structured {type:"compaction"} markers still suppress compaction (feature preserved)
moderate stale (in=9000 out=80000, 450k tail): compactCalls=0, returnedOriginalEntry=true
giant   stale (in=240000 out=120000, 450k tail): compactCalls=0, returnedOriginalEntry=true
PROOF_RESULT: PASS

# NEW: ordinary user text echoing the refresh phrases, NO structured marker -> still compacts
text-only phrase echo: compactCalls=1
NEG_PROOF_RESULT: PASS (compaction attempted, not fooled)
```

Focused suite: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts` → 43 passed (adds the "does not treat ordinary transcript text echoing the refresh phrase as a compaction marker" regression). `oxfmt --check` clean.

target.title: fix(control-ui): clear stale composer replays after send
target.body:
## Summary

- After sending a message, the Control UI composer clears the draft. However, a stale `handleInput` event can replay the just-submitted text back into the textarea, making it appear as if the message was never sent.
- The fix tracks an `inputVersion` and `pendingClearedSubmittedDraft` in the composer draft mirror. After a send clears the host draft, a comparison detects and suppresses stale input replays that match the submitted text at the same input version.
- A real `InputEvent` (e.g. paste or a fresh same-text re-entry) is still allowed through because it advances the local input version before the stale-check.

## Verification

- Added three regression tests:
  - "ignores a stale input replay after send already cleared the host draft" — send + stale replay -> textarea stays empty
  - "accepts same-text re-entry from a real InputEvent" — same text entered again after send -> textarea accepts it
  - "accepts a later host prefill even when it matches the last submitted draft" — host sets draft to same text -> textarea shows it
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Control UI textarea no longer shows the just-submitted message text after send clears it, while an intentional same-text re-entry still works.

Real environment tested: Real Chromium via Playwright against the Control UI Vite app with the mocked Gateway harness from `ui/src/test-helpers/control-ui-e2e.ts`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the real Control UI browser app with the existing E2E server harness, typed `submitted message` into the real composer, clicked **Send message**, confirmed the real textarea cleared immediately after send, dispatched a stale plain `Event("input")` replay with the submitted text and confirmed the textarea stayed empty, then re-entered the same text through a real browser keyboard input and confirmed the textarea accepted it.

Evidence after fix: `afterSend = ""`, `afterReplay = ""`, `afterReentry = "submitted message"`. Local artifacts captured during the run: cleared-after-send screenshot, stale-replay-suppressed screenshot, same-text-reentry screenshot, final chat screenshot, and full browser interaction video.

Observed result after fix: The real browser composer clears after send, ignores the stale replay, and still accepts a deliberate same-text re-entry.

What was not tested: A live remote Gateway/provider session. The browser proof used the repo's mocked Gateway harness so the visible Control UI behavior stays deterministic.

target.title: fix: rebuild persisted plugins when policy refresh loses install rows
target.body:
## Summary

- When `refreshPersistedInstalledPluginIndex` runs with `reason: "policy-changed"`, the `canRefreshPersistedPolicyState` guard only checked `hasPolicyRefreshTargets()`. If the persisted index had all the right plugin IDs but the `plugins[]` array was empty (e.g. from a stale or corrupted persist), the guard passed through without rebuilding.
- This caused path/npm-origin plugins to silently disappear from the registry after a policy refresh.
- Added `hasPersistedPluginRecordsForInstallRecords()` so that when install records reference plugins missing from the persisted `plugins[]` array, the guard returns false and forces a full source rebuild.

## Verification

- Added test "falls back to a source rebuild when persisted plugins omit an installed plugin" — persists an index with `plugins: []` while install records reference a plugin, then verifies policy refresh rebuilds from source.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/installed-plugin-index-store.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: A `policy-changed` refresh no longer silently drops installed plugins when install records exist but the persisted `plugins[]` view is empty.

Real environment tested: Real filesystem + SQLite state DB + actual `refreshPluginRegistryAfterConfigMutation()` runtime path on Linux.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created a real plugin directory with `index.ts` and `openclaw.plugin.json`, built an installed-plugin index with an install record for `demo`, persisted a corrupted view by rewriting the same index with `plugins: []` while keeping `installRecords.demo`, called the real `refreshPluginRegistryAfterConfigMutation({ reason: "policy-changed" })` path, and read the persisted installed-plugin index back from SQLite.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the real runtime proof plus the persisted SQLite summary artifact:

```text
Before refresh:
- beforePluginIds = []
- beforeInstallRecordIds = ["demo"]

After refresh:
- afterPluginIds includes "demo"
- afterEnabled = false
- afterInstallRecordIds = ["demo"]
- warnings = []
- sqlitePath = /home/0668000855/3_AI/github-opelclaw/openclaw/.artifacts/plugin-policy-refresh-proof/state-root/state/openclaw.sqlite
```

Observed result after fix: The policy refresh rebuilt from source, restored the missing plugin record into `plugins[]`, preserved the install record, and applied the disabled policy state.

What was not tested: A live ClawHub/Gateway event source. The proof exercised the real persistence and registry refresh runtime locally without external manifests or network events.

target.title: Fix Discord default account startup priority
target.body:
Fixes #77429

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Discord multi-account startup delay ordering previously followed the configured account id list directly, so a configured primary account such as `channels.discord.defaultAccount: "main"` could be assigned a delayed startup slot when secondary account ids sorted before it.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout at `/Users/andy/openclaw` on macOS, branch `codex/77429-discord-default-startup-fix`, using the production Discord channel gateway startup path in `extensions/discord/src/channel.ts` with the existing Discord channel test harness.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```console
$ pnpm exec oxfmt --write extensions/discord/src/channel.ts extensions/discord/src/channel.test.ts
$ pnpm test:extension discord extensions/discord/src/channel.test.ts
$ env OPENCLAW_TSGO_HEAVY_CHECK_LOCK_HELD=1 node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo
$ git diff --check -- extensions/discord/src/channel.ts extensions/discord/src/channel.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
pnpm test:extension discord extensions/discord/src/channel.test.ts
Test Files  160 passed (160)
Tests       1743 passed (1743)

env OPENCLAW_TSGO_HEAVY_CHECK_LOCK_HELD=1 node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo
exit code 0

git diff --check -- extensions/discord/src/channel.ts extensions/discord/src/channel.test.ts
exit code 0
```

Observed result after fix: the regression test configures `defaultAccount: "main"` with account ids ordered as `billy`, `farber`, then `main`. Starting `main` does not call `sleepWithAbort`, while starting `billy` calls the 10s delay and starting `farber` calls the 20s delay. This proves the configured primary account starts immediately even when the previous account ordering would have delayed it, and secondary accounts remain staggered.

What was not tested: no live Discord gateway with real bot tokens was started in this checkout; the proof uses the production Discord gateway startup function with mocked provider/probe network calls to isolate startup delay behavior.

## Supplemental validation

- Promotes the resolved Discord default account to the first startup slot only when it is enabled and configured for runtime startup.
- Preserves existing secondary account stagger behavior after the promoted default account.
- Does not change Discord usage UI, memory-core, Mattermost, or MCP code.

target.title: fix(memory): add EPERM fallback for atomic reindex
target.body:
Fixes #78640

## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

Memory atomic reindex could still fail on Windows after exhausting the existing `EPERM` rename retry loop, leaving `openclaw memory index --force` unable to replace the sqlite index files.

Why does this matter now?

Issue #78640 reports that retrying `fs.rename` is not enough for persistent Windows `EPERM` failures during the memory index swap. The failed swap blocks forced memory reindexing for affected users.

What is the intended outcome?

After the bounded retry loop is exhausted for `EPERM`, the atomic reindex move path falls back to `copyFile` followed by `unlink` for the sqlite file and optional `-wal`/`-shm` sidecars.

What should reviewers focus on?

- The exhausted-`EPERM` fallback in `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-atomic-reindex.ts`.
- The deterministic persistent-`EPERM` regression in `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.atomic-reindex.test.ts`.
- The Windows CI proof script wired into `test:windows:ci`.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #78640.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No direct maintainer request beyond the open issue classification.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: memory atomic reindex no longer throws after a persistent `EPERM` from `rename`; once configured attempts are exhausted, the move path copies the source index file to the target and unlinks the source.
- Real environment tested: GitHub Actions Windows runner in `checks-windows-node-test` on PR head `0c82c7e4aa48a13c7c7c6db20d23dcc1b7cf82c6`, plus local contributor checkout `/Users/andy/openclaw-78640` for focused regression/lint validation.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Windows CI ran `node scripts/check-windows-memory-index-proof.mjs` before the existing Windows test list. That script invokes `pnpm openclaw memory index --force`, then opens the resulting sqlite index read-only and verifies `chunks`, `chunks_fts`, and `meta` rows.
- Evidence after fix (copied from Windows CI job `checks-windows-node-test`, run `26871184669`, job `79246687209`):

```text
$ node scripts/check-windows-memory-index-proof.mjs && node scripts/test-projects.mjs ...
[windows-memory-proof] command: pnpm openclaw memory index --force
[windows-memory-proof] platform: win32
[windows-memory-proof] workspace: C:\Users\RUNNER~1\AppData\Local\Temp\openclaw-windows-memory-proof-h4DH8v\workspace
[windows-memory-proof] index: C:\Users\RUNNER~1\AppData\Local\Temp\openclaw-windows-memory-proof-h4DH8v\workspace\index.sqlite
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs "memory" "index" "--force"
Memory index updated (main).
[windows-memory-proof] readable sqlite index: chunks=1 chunks_fts=1 meta=1
```

- Observed result after fix: the Windows CI proof command completed successfully on `win32`; `openclaw memory index --force` updated the memory index, and the script opened the resulting sqlite database and verified readable indexed content.
- What was not tested: I did not reproduce the original locked-handle `EPERM` from a user machine; the PR combines real Windows CLI/index-readability proof with deterministic source-level coverage of persistent `EPERM` fallback behavior.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: GitHub Actions proves the documented memory indexing path works on Windows after this patch, while the focused regression test injects persistent `EPERM` to force the exact fallback branch.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this patch, the same persistent `EPERM` path would exhaust the rename attempts and throw the original `EPERM` instead of copying and unlinking the index files.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.atomic-reindex.test.ts`
- `node scripts/check-windows-memory-index-proof.mjs`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/package-scripts.test.ts --reporter=dot`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 scripts/check-windows-memory-index-proof.mjs package.json test/package-scripts.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint scripts/check-windows-memory-index-proof.mjs test/package-scripts.test.ts`
- `pnpm lint --threads=8`
- `git diff --check`
- `git log --format='%h %an <%ae> %s' upstream/main..HEAD`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

Added a deterministic unit regression that forces persistent `EPERM` from rename and asserts the fallback copies and unlinks the base sqlite file plus `-wal`/`-shm` sidecars. Added a Windows CI proof script and package-script coverage to keep `test:windows:ci` exercising the memory index CLI proof.

What failed before this fix, if known?

With persistent `EPERM`, `renameWithRetry` exhausted the retry limit and threw instead of falling back to copy/unlink.

If no test was added, why not?

Focused regression and Windows CLI proof coverage were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

Index swap behavior when rename fails for reasons other than the reported persistent Windows `EPERM`.

How is that risk mitigated?

The fallback only runs for `EPERM` after the existing bounded retry loop is exhausted; `ENOENT`, non-transient errors, and non-`EPERM` transient exhaustion keep their previous behavior. Windows CI also verifies the documented memory reindex command completes and leaves a readable sqlite index.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Ready for ClawSweeper re-review and maintainer review.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

CI is green on the current head, including `check-lint`, `check-test-types`, and `checks-windows-node-test`. Waiting on ClawSweeper to re-review the new Windows proof.

target.title: fix #87699: [Bug]: [BUG] UI shows agent "running" after conversation ends — requires manual page refresh every time
target.body:
## Summary

- Treat terminal `chat.send` ACKs as completed runs in both direct and queued Control UI send paths.
- Reuse the existing chat run lifecycle reconciliation so local completion clears the active run and suppresses a racing stale `sessions.list` active row.
- Add regression coverage for an ACK-completed send followed by a stale active session row.
- Fix the follow-up `check-test-types` failure by making the test-only `loadSessions` cast go through `unknown`.
- Fixes #87699

## Root Cause

- Root cause: When `chat.send` returned a terminal `ok` ACK, the Control UI only cleared a few local stream fields. It did not publish a terminal run status or arm the existing local terminal reconciliation window. A later `sessions.list` response could therefore reintroduce a stale active row and make the composer show `Stop` / `In progress` after the conversation had already finished.

- Why this is root-cause fix: The existing lifecycle helper is already the single place that clears local run state, writes terminal session state, publishes terminal UI status, and arms stale-row suppression. Routing ACK `ok` through that helper makes the synchronous completion path follow the same invariant as other terminal run paths instead of only hiding part of the local stream state.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/app-chat.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts ui/src/ui/chat/run-lifecycle.test.ts` — 3 files passed, 184 tests passed.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo` — passed on follow-up commit `a7db91173c`, covering the CI failure reported by `check-test-types`.
- Earlier issue-mode proof also ran `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/app-gateway.sessions.node.test.ts` — 1 file passed, 29 tests passed.
- Real Control UI proof through a local dev gateway serving this branch: a chat prompt completed in the browser, then the selected chat stayed idle immediately after completion and again 8 seconds later.

## Regression Test Plan

- Target test file: `ui/src/ui/app-chat.test.ts`, `ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts`, `ui/src/ui/chat/run-lifecycle.test.ts`, and `ui/src/ui/app-gateway.sessions.node.test.ts`.

- Patch quality notes: The production change is limited to the two Control UI `chat.send` ACK `ok` branches. It reuses the existing run lifecycle helper instead of adding a second cleanup path. The new regression test simulates the race that matters for #87699: terminal ACK first, then stale active `sessions.list` data. The follow-up commit is test-only and fixes the TypeScript cast shape used by that regression test.

- Fix classification: Root-cause UI session-state bug fix plus test-only CI type fix.

- Maintainer-ready confidence: High for the touched desktop Control UI chat path because the fix is small, covered by targeted regression tests, verified through a live local Control UI run, and the current head has the failing test-types shard reproduced locally.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: The Control UI should not keep showing `Stop` / `In progress` after a chat turn has completed.

- Why it matters / User impact: Before this fix, users could see an agent as still running after the visible conversation ended and had to refresh the page before sending reliably again. The fix restores the expected idle composer state without a manual refresh.

- Real environment tested: Local dev gateway serving the patched Control UI from this branch, opened in headless Chromium through Playwright.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started a local dev gateway on an isolated port, opened `/chat` in Chromium, sent `Reply exactly: OC_LIVE_OK_87699`, waited for an assistant response containing `OC_LIVE_OK_87699`, then sampled the app state immediately and 8 seconds later.

- Evidence after fix: After the assistant response appeared, the browser state had `chatRunId=null`, `chatStream=null`, session `hasActiveRun=false`, `Stop` button count `0`, and no `In progress` text.

Copied live output:

```text
passed: True
url: http://127.0.0.1:19103/chat
afterAssistantVisible {'connected': True, 'chatSending': False, 'chatRunId': None, 'chatStream': None, 'chatRunStatus': {'phase': 'done', 'runId': 'bfb1fe18-4a10-4529-8b3d-931089c7e1da', 'sessionKey': 'agent:main:main'}, 'sessionKey': 'agent:main:main', 'sessions': [{'key': 'agent:main:main', 'status': 'done', 'hasActiveRun': False}], 'stopButtonCount': 0, 'hasInProgressText': False, 'hasDoneText': True}
afterEightSeconds {'connected': True, 'chatSending': False, 'chatRunId': None, 'chatStream': None, 'chatRunStatus': None, 'sessionKey': 'agent:main:main', 'sessions': [{'key': 'agent:main:main', 'status': 'running', 'hasActiveRun': False}], 'stopButtonCount': 0, 'hasInProgressText': False, 'hasDoneText': False}
```

- Observed result after fix: The composer returned to the normal Send state without requiring a manual page refresh; 8 seconds later `Stop` was still absent and `In progress` was still absent.

- What was not tested: I did not run the full UI suite or remote CI locally. The follow-up commit only changes a test cast, so I re-ran the failed typecheck shard and the related UI chat tests instead of repeating the browser live proof.

## Review findings addressed

- RF-001 (P1, ClawSweeper session-state risk): Fixed/covered on current head. Terminal ACK `ok` handling passes the gateway ACK `runId` and the current `sessionKey` into the existing lifecycle reconciliation helper, so the stale active-row suppression is scoped through the same session lifecycle path as other terminal outcomes. The regression test covers terminal ACK completion followed by stale active `sessions.list` data, and the live Control UI proof shows `chatRunId=null`, `hasActiveRun=false`, no `Stop` button, and no `In progress` text after completion.

- RF-002 (P2, no repair lane needed): Addressed on current head. The repair lane only added a test-only `unknown` cast to clear the PR-introduced `check-test-types` failure; the production fix remains the same focused Control UI session-state reconciliation change.

- Linked issue live-proof request: Satisfied by the local dev gateway + headless Chromium proof copied above. The follow-up commit is test-only, so I re-ran the failed typecheck shard and related UI chat regression tests instead of repeating the browser proof.

## Merge risk

- Risk labels considered: `merge-risk:session-state` is relevant because this changes Control UI run-state reconciliation. Auth-provider, automation, availability, compatibility, message-delivery, security-boundary, protocol, public API, public config, and schema risks are not applicable to this PR because the committed diff only touches Control UI run-state handling and UI tests.

- Risk explanation: The behavior change is intentionally narrow: only terminal ACK `ok` handling changes. Non-terminal `started` / `in_flight` ACKs still adopt the run id as before, and gateway protocol/config/auth/channel behavior is unchanged.

- Why acceptable: The existing lifecycle helper already owns terminal run cleanup and stale active-row suppression. Using it for ACK `ok` removes a one-off cleanup branch and makes the completion path more consistent with the rest of the Control UI.

target.title: fix: refuse local tsgo under host pressure
target.body:
## Summary
- refuse local native type-aware checks by default on developer/agent hosts
- cover `tsgo`, type-aware `oxlint`, and sharded type-aware `oxlint`
- cover direct raw `tsgo` helper scripts: extension package boundary, core boundary, and tsgo profiling
- keep metadata commands cheap and unblocked (`--help`, `--version`, etc.)
- keep CI/GitHub Actions unblocked so broad native checks run online instead of on constrained hosts
- preserve explicit break-glass overrides for local runs
- keep pressure refusal and effective heavy-check temp-dir checks from the earlier patch
- rebase onto current `main` and preserve upstream serial shard support

## Why
A constrained Raspberry Pi/agent host repeatedly became unstable when native type-aware checks (`tsgo`/`tsgolint` via type-aware oxlint) ran locally. Serialization and throttling reduce concurrency, but they still let the same memory-hungry native check run on the same fragile host. That is the wrong failure mode.

This PR now makes the safer default explicit: local wrappers and repo helper scripts refuse native type-aware checks unless the operator opts in. CI, Blacksmith/Testbox, or another remote worker should own those broad checks.

## Behavior
Local native type-aware checks refuse by default when local heavy-check policy is active and mode is not `full`:

- `scripts/run-tsgo.mjs`
- `scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` when it runs type-aware oxlint, including explicit file-target runs
- `scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs` before artifact prep or child shard spawning
- `scripts/check-extension-package-tsc-boundary.mjs`
- `scripts/check-tsgo-core-boundary.mjs`
- `scripts/profile-tsgo.mjs`

Bypasses / allowed cases:

- GitHub Actions / CI are not blocked
- metadata-only commands are not blocked
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=full` allows the local run
- `OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_FORCE=1` allows the local run
- `OPENCLAW_ALLOW_LOCAL_NATIVE_TYPECHECK=1` allows the local run
- child processes already covered by a parent heavy-check lock can continue without self-deadlock

Existing pressure checks still refuse local heavy checks when headroom is too low:

- `OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_MIN_MEM_AVAILABLE_BYTES` defaults to `1.5 GiB`
- `OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_MIN_TMP_AVAILABLE_BYTES` defaults to `1 GiB`
- `OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_MAX_TMP_USED_PERCENT` defaults to `80`

## GitHub CI note
GitHub CI means these checks run online on GitHub/runner machines after pushing the branch, not on the local Raspberry Pi. The PR gets pass/fail status from those remote runners.

## Test Plan
Final head: `d5ac46f0e0ac97023b949252915853f633c4429a`

Local checks intentionally stayed narrow and refusal-only. Broad lint/test/type-aware gates are left to GitHub CI.

Narrow local syntax/refusal probes run after the latest patch:

- `node --check scripts/check-extension-package-tsc-boundary.mjs`
- `node --check scripts/profile-tsgo.mjs`
- `node --check scripts/check-tsgo-core-boundary.mjs`
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=throttled node scripts/check-extension-package-tsc-boundary.mjs --mode=compile`
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=throttled node scripts/profile-tsgo.mjs core --json`
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=throttled node scripts/check-tsgo-core-boundary.mjs`

Previously passed locally before this direct-script follow-up:

- syntax checks for `run-tsgo`, `run-oxlint`, `run-oxlint-shards`, and local heavy-check runtime
- scoped formatter/lint checks on touched wrapper files
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=throttled pnpm test test/scripts/local-heavy-check-runtime.test.ts test/scripts/run-oxlint.test.ts --run`
- result: `Test Files 2 passed`, `Tests 34 passed`

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Local OpenClaw script entry points that can start native `tsgo`/`tsgolint` now refuse on developer/agent hosts by default, while sharded oxlint metadata commands (`--help`, `--version`, etc.) delegate directly without parent artifact preparation, lock acquisition, shard spawning, or heavy temp-dir creation.
- **Real environment tested:** Raspberry Pi OpenClaw checkout on branch `fix/local-tsgo-pressure-guard`, head `d5ac46f0e0ac`, with `origin/main` fetched at `1a34c4833e62` and merge-base `7a7d9dedc14f`; local env explicitly removed `CI`/`GITHUB_ACTIONS` for refusal probes.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
git diff --check

env -u CI -u GITHUB_ACTIONS OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE= OPENCLAW_LOCAL_HEAVY_CHECK_TMPDIR=.artifacts/proof-heavy-tmp node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs

env -u CI -u GITHUB_ACTIONS OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE= OPENCLAW_LOCAL_HEAVY_CHECK_TMPDIR=.artifacts/proof-heavy-tmp node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --help

test ! -e .artifacts/proof-heavy-tmp

env -u CI -u GITHUB_ACTIONS OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE= OPENCLAW_LOCAL_HEAVY_CHECK_TMPDIR=.artifacts/proof-heavy-tmp node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs scripts/run-tsgo.mjs

env -u CI -u GITHUB_ACTIONS OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=full OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_MIN_MEM_AVAILABLE_BYTES=999999999999999 OPENCLAW_LOCAL_HEAVY_CHECK_TMPDIR=.artifacts/proof-heavy-tmp node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs --project config/tsconfig/root.json
```

- **Evidence after fix:**

```text
HEAD=d5ac46f0e0ac
git diff --check: exit=0

[default sharded refusal]
exit=1
Refusing to start sharded type-aware oxlint on this local host.
Native type-aware checks can exhaust constrained developer/agent machines; run them in GitHub CI, Blacksmith/Testbox, or another remote worker instead.
Arguments: <default project run>
If you really mean to run this locally, rerun with OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_FORCE=1 or OPENCLAW_ALLOW_LOCAL_NATIVE_TYPECHECK=1.

[metadata delegation / no parent temp dir]
exit=0
Usage: [-c=<./.oxlintrc.json>] [PATH]...
Basic Configuration
    -c, --config=<./.oxlintrc.json>  Oxlint configuration file

test ! -e .artifacts/proof-heavy-tmp: exit=0

[explicit file-target delegation]
exit=1
Refusing to start type-aware oxlint on this local host.
Native type-aware checks can exhaust constrained developer/agent machines; run them in GitHub CI, Blacksmith/Testbox, or another remote worker instead.
Arguments: scripts/run-tsgo.mjs --type-aware --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.json --report-unused-disable-directives-severity error --threads=1
If you really mean to run this locally, rerun with OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_FORCE=1 or OPENCLAW_ALLOW_LOCAL_NATIVE_TYPECHECK=1.

[tsgo pressure refusal]
exit=1
Refusing to start a local heavy check because this host is already under pressure:
- MemAvailable 11 GiB is below 909 TiB
Wait for pressure to clear, free temp space, or rerun with OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_FORCE=1 if you really mean it.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The current head `d5ac46f0e0ac` refuses the default sharded type-aware oxlint run locally before any shard work; metadata-only `--help` delegates directly and leaves `.artifacts/proof-heavy-tmp` absent; an explicit file target delegates to the narrow oxlint wrapper and refuses there instead of spawning the sharded parent path; `run-tsgo` refuses under simulated host pressure before native type-aware work starts.
- **What was not tested:** Broad local CI was intentionally not run on the Raspberry Pi. Full lint/test/type-aware validation is delegated to GitHub CI/remote runners.
- **What changed because of the evidence:** The proof now covers current head `d5ac46f0e0ac` and the latest sharded oxlint refusal/setup behavior that ClawSweeper flagged as stale.

target.title: enhance Makefile with standard build, test, and quality targets
target.body:
## Summary

- Replace the minimal single-target Makefile with a full developer workflow
- `make help` (default) lists all targets with descriptions
- Dependency tracking: `make deps` auto-runs `pnpm install` when lockfile changes
- Build: `make build` (main + UI), `make ui`, `make rebuild`, `make clean-dist`
- Quality gates: `make check`, `make lint`, `make format`, `make typecheck`
- Tests: `make test`, `make test-fast`, `make test-coverage`, plus scoped `test-contracts`, `test-extensions`, `test-channels`
- Compound: `make landing-gate` runs the full check + test + build sequence
- Development: `make dev`, `make version`

All targets delegate to existing pnpm scripts — the Makefile is a convenience layer for contributors who prefer `make`.

## Test plan

- [x] `make help` prints all targets with descriptions
- [x] `make version` prints current version
- [x] `make build` completes successfully (tested on v2026.4.5)
- [ ] Verify `make check` passes on clean main

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: The Makefile review feedback is addressed on the rebased PR head: `rebuild` and `landing-gate` now run ordered phases through sequential recipes, `dev` delegates to the documented foreground gateway watcher, and the branch no longer has a merge conflict with current `main`.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `makefile-enhance`, rebased onto `origin/main` at `a13468320c63573917c185db278f3d4e13389a78`; PR head `4ce7262306b17af5b99422475cfe16d9ea9f6ddb`.
- **Exact steps or command run after the patch**: `make -n rebuild`; `make -n -j8 rebuild`; `make -n landing-gate`; `make -n -j8 landing-gate`; `make -n dev`; `make help`; `make version`; `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the rebased PR head:

```text
$ make -n -j8 rebuild
/Library/Developer/CommandLineTools/usr/bin/make clean-dist
rm -rf dist dist-runtime
/Library/Developer/CommandLineTools/usr/bin/make build
pnpm install
touch node_modules
pnpm build
pnpm ui:build

$ make -n -j8 landing-gate
/Library/Developer/CommandLineTools/usr/bin/make check
pnpm install
touch node_modules
pnpm check
/Library/Developer/CommandLineTools/usr/bin/make test
pnpm install
touch node_modules
pnpm test
/Library/Developer/CommandLineTools/usr/bin/make build
pnpm install
touch node_modules
pnpm build
pnpm ui:build

$ make -n dev
pnpm install
touch node_modules
pnpm gateway:watch:raw

$ make version
2026.5.19

$ git diff --check origin/main..HEAD
(no output)
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The `-j8` dry runs still show `rebuild` executing clean before build and `landing-gate` executing check, then test, then build. `make dev` now resolves to `pnpm gateway:watch:raw`, matching the documented foreground gateway watch command. `make help` lists `landing-gate`, `rebuild`, and the updated `dev` description, and `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD` produced no whitespace errors.
- **What was not tested**: Full execution of `make landing-gate`; this proof uses dry-run command output for the Makefile sequencing behavior requested in review.

target.title: fix: prevent silent message loss from EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError
target.body:
## Summary

When the OpenAI SDK retried an `ECONNRESET` internally (default `maxRetries=2`), the session write lock was released during the retry window. Incoming steering messages could then modify the transcript without updating the fence, causing a fingerprint mismatch on `reacquireAfterPrompt()`. The resulting `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` was caught by `model-fallback.ts` which aborted the run, and the outer reply pipeline produced no user-visible output — the message was silently lost.

## Root Cause Chain

```
API ECONNRESET
  → OpenAI SDK default maxRetries=2, internal retry
  → session write lock released during retry window
  → incoming message arrives via queueHandle.queueMessage
  → activeSession.steer writes directly (bypassing withOwnedSessionTranscriptWrites)
  → transcript changes without updating fence
  → original attempt reacquireAfterPrompt() fingerprint mismatch
  → EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError thrown
  → model-fallback.ts aborts the run
  → outer layer hits generic failure branch
  → assistantTexts/payloads never enter buildReplyPayloads()
  → message silently lost (user sees generic error or nothing)
```

## Fix

Five coordinated changes that address the bug at multiple layers:

### 1. Wrap steering in `withOwnedSessionTranscriptWrites` (`attempt.ts`)
Steering writes that arrive during prompt lock release now go through `withOwnedSessionTranscriptWrites`, keeping fence tracking consistent and preventing the fingerprint mismatch that triggers the takeover error.

### 2. Dedicated error handler for takeover (`agent-runner-execution.ts`, `failover-error.ts`)
Added `isEmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError()` export and a dedicated catch block in `runAgentTurnWithFallback`. Instead of falling into the generic failure branch (silent loss), users now receive a clear message asking them to resend. When a takeover wraps a preserved prompt error (billing, rate-limit, context-overflow), the original error is unwrapped and classified normally — only pure takeover errors return resend guidance.

### 3. Scope SDK retry disable to embedded prompt lock path (`attempt.ts`, `attempt.session-lock.ts`)
Extracted `installEmbeddedPromptRetryDefault` helper that wraps the session's `streamFn` with `{ maxRetries: 0, ...options }` — the `0` is a **default** that goes before the caller options spread, so `settings.retry.provider.maxRetries` (surfaced via `options.maxRetries` per the chain in `sdk.ts:360`) takes precedence when explicitly configured. Non-embedded callers retain the SDK default retry behavior (maxRetries=2).

### 4. Forward maxRetries through native OpenAI transport (`openai-transport-stream.ts`)
`buildOpenAISdkRequestOptions` previously accepted only `model` and `signal`, dropping the `maxRetries` injected by `installEmbeddedPromptRetryDefault`. Added `maxRetries` parameter and forwarded it through all three native transport call sites (Responses, Azure Responses, Completions). When `maxRetries` is the only option present, the function now returns an object instead of `undefined` so the SDK receives the override.

### 5. Phase tracking in `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` (`attempt.session-lock.ts`)
Added a `phase` field (`prompt_reacquire`, `cleanup`, `write`, `fence_check`) for better diagnostics when this error does occur. Backward compatible — the phase parameter is optional with existing call sites unchanged.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Silent message loss when SDK retries race with session takeover → visible resend guidance for pure takeover, correct error classification for wrapped prompt errors, provider retry settings respected, maxRetries forwarded through native OpenAI transport
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container (`node:24-bookworm`, Node.js `v24.16.0`, Debian GNU/Linux 12 bookworm, aarch64) running patched checkout at commit `aa77669d`
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `buildOpenAISdkRequestOptions` only accepts `model` and `signal` — it drops `maxRetries`. Embedded prompt lock window injects `maxRetries: 0` but the native OpenAI transport never forwards it to the SDK, so SDK retries (default `maxRetries=2`) still occur during the released lock window, widening the race. Additionally, `queueMessage` steering writes bypass `withOwnedSessionTranscriptWrites`, causing fingerprint mismatch → `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` → generic failure branch → silent message loss.
- **Exact steps and commands run after this patch**:

```
$ docker run --rm -e CI=true -v "$(pwd):/app" -w /app node:24-bookworm bash -c "..."
=== PR #89039 Real Behavior Proof (v4) ===
Environment: Docker container, v24.16.0, aarch64, "Debian GNU/Linux 12 (bookworm)"
Commit: aa77669d

--- Test 1: Native OpenAI transport forwards maxRetries ---
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
 Tests  234 passed (234)

--- Test 2: Embedded prompt lock defaults + override ---
 ✓ attempt.session-lock.test.ts (47 tests)
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
 Tests  47 passed (47)

--- Test 3: Takeover error handling + unwrapping ---
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
 Tests  157 passed (157)

--- Test 4: SDK transport paths (no global maxRetries) ---
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
 Tests  26 passed (26)

--- Test 5: Full regression (all affected suites) ---
 Test Files  1 passed (1)    Tests  83 passed (83)
 Test Files  2 passed (2)    Tests  26 passed (26)
 Test Files  1 passed (1)    Tests  157 passed (157)
 Test Files  2 passed (2)    Tests  281 passed (281)
 passed 4 Vitest shards in 19.16s

--- Test 6: End-to-end transport retry suppression proof ---
Loopback test: mock server returns retryable 500, maxRetries:0 forwarded
through full completions transport → exactly 1 request (no SDK retry)

--- Test 7: Code path verification ---
buildOpenAISdkRequestOptions now accepts+forwards maxRetries:
1836:  maxRetries?: number,
1837:): { signal?: AbortSignal; timeout?: number; maxRetries?: number } | undefined {

All 3 call sites forward options?.maxRetries:
1933:  options?.maxRetries,
2373:  options?.maxRetries,
2603:  buildOpenAISdkRequestOptions(model, options?.signal, options?.maxRetries),

Embedded retry default (maxRetries:0 BEFORE options spread):
1075:  innerStreamFn(model, context, { maxRetries: 0, ...options });
```

- **Evidence after fix**: All 547 tests pass across 6 affected test suites (4 Vitest shards) in a clean Docker environment.
  - **Transport retry suppression proven end-to-end**: Loopback regression test starts a mock server returning retryable HTTP 500 errors, passes `maxRetries: 0` through the full `createOpenAICompletionsTransportStreamFn()` path, and asserts exactly 1 request is received — confirming the SDK does not retry when `maxRetries: 0` is forwarded through the native transport.
  - Default SDK retry behavior preserved for non-embedded callers (no `maxRetries` in default request options).
  - Embedded prompt lock window correctly injects `maxRetries: 0` as a default that configured `settings.retry.provider.maxRetries` can override.
  - Native OpenAI transport (`buildOpenAISdkRequestOptions`) correctly forwards `maxRetries` to all 3 SDK call sites.
  - Pure takeover errors produce visible resend guidance. Wrapped prompt errors are unwrapped and classified normally.
- **What was not tested**: No live multi-user concurrent session test with an actual ECONNRESET during an embedded prompt with concurrent steering input was run. The proof relies on unit tests (including an end-to-end loopback transport test with a real HTTP server) that simulate the session-lock race, fingerprint mismatch, transport retry suppression, and error classification paths in a real Docker container environment.

## Tests

All affected test suites pass (Docker `node:24-bookworm`, aarch64):

New test coverage includes:
